preamble.summary: Chapter 7 (following Hebrew versification, beginning at 7:2 in English Bibles) contains the final and most daring wasf — a foot-to-head praise of the woman's body as she dances. The man describes her feet, thighs, navel, belly, breasts, neck, eyes, nose, and head with increasingly bold imagery. The chapter climaxes with the woman's triumphant declaration: 'I am my beloved's and his desire is upon me' — the final evolution of the mutual-possession formula. She invites him to the countryside, the vineyards, and the fields where she will give him her love.
preamble.remarkable: This wasf reverses the direction of chapter 4: there he began with her head and moved down; here he begins with her feet and moves up. The reversal suggests she is dancing — the observers' eyes naturally move upward from her sandaled feet. The metaphors are also bolder: her navel is a round goblet that never lacks mixed wine, her belly is a heap of wheat fringed with lilies, her breasts are like clusters of grapes he desires to grasp. The woman's declaration in verse 11 (Hebrew) is the Song's theological summit: ani le-dodi ve-alay teshuqato ('I am my beloved's and his desire is upon me'). The word teshuqah ('desire') appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible — here, in Genesis 3:16 (the woman's desire for her husband after the fall), and Genesis 4:7 (sin's desire for Cain). The Song reverses Genesis 3: there, desire was linked to domination; here, desire is mutual and free.
preamble.friction: The boldness of the body imagery has always made commentators uncomfortable. The navel-as-wine-goblet and belly-as-wheat-heap are the Song's most explicitly physical descriptions, and attempts to allegorize them reveal the discomfort more than they resolve it. The Hebrew in verse 1 describes her dancing thighs and the 'curves of her thighs' (chammuqe yerekayikh), which is frank anatomical description. We preserve the text's directness.
preamble.connections: The teshuqah ('desire') in verse 11 explicitly reverses Genesis 3:16, where the same word describes the woman's desire bound to her husband's rule. Here the same desire operates without domination — the fall's distortion of eros is undone. The vineyard and field imagery of verses 12-14 return to the Song's opening themes (1:6, 2:15) and anticipate the final vineyard declaration in 8:12. The mandrakes (duda'im) in verse 14 echo Genesis 30:14-16, where Rachel and Leah negotiate with mandrakes for access to Jacob — an aphrodisiac plant linked to fertility and desire.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־יָּפ֧וּ פְעָמַ֛יִךְ בַּנְּעָלִ֖ים בַּת־נָדִ֑יב חַמּוּקֵ֣י יְרֵכַ֔יִךְ כְּמ֣וֹ חֲלָאִ֔ים מַעֲשֵׂ֖ה יְדֵ֥י אָמָּֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: How beautiful are thy feet with shoes, O prince's daughter! the joints of thy thighs are like jewels, the work of the hands of a cunning workman.
verses.rendering:
How beautiful are your sandaled feet,
O daughter of nobles!
The curves of your thighs are like ornaments,
the work of a master craftsman.
translator_notes: The wasf begins at the bottom — pe'amayikh ba-ne'alim ('your feet in sandals'). She is dancing, and the observers' eyes start at her moving feet. Bat nadiv ('daughter of a noble/prince') gives her aristocratic status. Chammuqe yerekayikh ('the curves of your thighs') is frank anatomical praise — the smooth, rounded contours of her upper legs are like chala'im ('ornaments'), as finely wrought as a master artisan's work.

key_terms.semantic_range: curves, roundings, turnings of the thighs/hips; the smooth contours of the upper leg
key_terms.note: Chammuqim (from chamaq, 'to turn, to curve') describes the flowing lines of her thighs. The same root appears in 5:6 where the beloved 'turns away.' Here the turning is physical beauty — the curves of a dancing body.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁרְרֵךְ֙ אַגַּ֣ן הַסַּ֔הַר אַל־יֶחְסַ֖ר הַמָּ֑זֶג בִּטְנֵךְ֙ עֲרֵמַ֣ת חִטִּ֔ים סוּגָ֖ה בַּשּׁוֹשַׁנִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy navel is like a round goblet, which wanteth not liquor: thy belly is like an heap of wheat set about with lilies.
verses.rendering:
Your navel is a rounded goblet —
may it never lack mixed wine!
Your belly is a mound of wheat
fringed with lilies.
translator_notes: Shorrerekh aggan ha-sahar ('your navel is a round/crescent goblet') — the comparison to a wine goblet that never runs dry is unmistakably sensual. Bitnekh ('your belly, your womb') is a heap of wheat (the golden-tan color of her skin) surrounded by lilies (shoshannim). The images are of abundance: wine that never empties, grain heaped high, flowers framing everything.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁנֵ֥י שָׁדַ֛יִךְ כִּשְׁנֵ֥י עֳפָרִ֖ים תָּאֳמֵ֥י צְבִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy two breasts are like two young roes that are twins.
verses.rendering:
Your two breasts are like two fawns,
twins of a gazelle.
translator_notes: Repeated from 4:5 with slight variation — the 'grazing among the lilies' clause is omitted here, focusing purely on the twin fawns. The repetition underscores the permanence of his admiration.

verses.text_hebrew: צַוָּארֵ֖ךְ כְּמִגְדַּ֣ל הַשֵּׁ֑ן עֵינַ֗יִךְ בְּרֵכוֹת֙ בְּחֶשְׁבּ֔וֹן עַל־שַׁ֖עַר בַּת־רַבִּ֑ים אַפֵּ֕ךְ כְּמִגְדַּ֥ל הַלְּבָנ֖וֹן צוֹפֶ֥ה פְּנֵ֥י דַמָּֽשֶׂק׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy neck is as a tower of ivory; thine eyes like the fishpools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bathrabbim: thy nose is as the tower of Lebanon which looketh toward Damascus.
verses.rendering:
Your neck is like a tower of ivory.
Your eyes are the pools of Heshbon
beside the gate of Bath-rabbim.
Your nose is like the tower of Lebanon
facing toward Damascus.
translator_notes: The neck moves from 'Tower of David' (4:4) to 'tower of ivory' (migdal ha-shen) — smooth, pale, and precious. Her eyes are berechot be-Cheshbon ('pools in Heshbon') — the still, deep pools near an ancient Transjordanian city. Her nose is ka-migdal ha-Levanon ('like the tower of Lebanon facing Damascus') — a watchtower on the heights, commanding and dignified.

key_terms.semantic_range: pools, reservoirs, fishponds; calm collected water
key_terms.note: The pools of Heshbon were famous in antiquity. Comparing her eyes to still, deep pools emphasizes depth, clarity, and calm beauty — water you can see into and lose yourself in.

verses.text_hebrew: רֹאשֵׁ֤ךְ עָלַ֙יִךְ֙ כַּכַּרְמֶ֔ל וְדַלַּ֥ת רֹאשֵׁ֖ךְ כָּאַרְגָּמָ֑ן מֶ֖לֶךְ אָס֥וּר בָּרְהָטִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine head upon thee is like Carmel, and the hair of thine head like purple; the king is held in the galleries.
verses.rendering:
Your head crowns you like Carmel,
and the flowing hair of your head is like purple —
a king is held captive in the tresses!
translator_notes: Her head is like Carmel — the great headland jutting into the Mediterranean, majestic and commanding. Her flowing hair (dallat roshekh) is like argaman ('purple') — the royal color. Then the astonishing conclusion: melekh asur ba-rehatim ('a king is captured/bound in the tresses'). Her hair is a trap that snares kings. His whole wasf concludes with his own captivity — he, the king, is a prisoner of her beauty.

verses.text_kjv: How fair and how pleasant art thou, O love, for delights!
verses.rendering:
How beautiful you are, how delightful —
O love, among pleasures!
translator_notes: Mah yafit u-mah na'amt ('how beautiful you are and how pleasant') — the superlatives overflow. Ahavah ba-ta'anugot ('love among delights/pleasures') addresses her not by name but as ahavah itself — she IS love, embodied.

verses.text_hebrew: זֹ֤את קוֹמָתֵךְ֙ דָּֽמְתָ֣ה לְתָמָ֔ר וְשָׁדַ֖יִךְ לְאַשְׁכֹּלֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: This thy stature is like to a palm tree, and thy breasts to clusters of grapes.
verses.rendering:
Your stature is like a palm tree,
and your breasts like clusters of fruit.
translator_notes: Qomatekh damtah le-tamar ('your height is like a palm tree') — the date palm is tall, straight, graceful, and fruitful. Her breasts are now compared to eshkolot ('clusters') — date clusters or grape clusters hanging from the palm. The shift from fawns to fruit clusters prepares for his intention in the next verse.

verses.text_hebrew: אָמַ֙רְתִּי֙ אֶעֱלֶ֣ה בְתָמָ֔ר אֹֽחֲזָ֖ה בְּסַנְסִנָּ֑יו וְיִֽהְיוּ־נָ֤א שָׁדַ֙יִךְ֙ כְּאֶשְׁכְּל֣וֹת הַגֶּ֔פֶן וְרֵ֥יחַ אַפֵּ֖ךְ כַּתַּפּוּחִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: I said, I will go up to the palm tree, I will take hold of the boughs thereof: now also thy breasts shall be as clusters of the vine, and the smell of thy nose like apples;
verses.rendering:
I said: 'I will climb the palm tree,
I will seize its branches!'
Let your breasts be like clusters of the vine,
the fragrance of your breath like apples,
translator_notes: Amarti e'eleh ve-tamar ('I said, I will climb the palm tree') — he will ascend her body as one climbs a date palm to harvest fruit. Ochazah be-sansinnav ('I will seize its branches/fronds') — achaz ('to seize') is the same verb the woman used in 3:4 when she seized her beloved. Now he seizes her. The climbing and grasping are explicitly sexual.

verses.text_hebrew: וְחִכֵּ֕ךְ כְּיֵ֥ין הַטּ֛וֹב הוֹלֵ֥ךְ לְדוֹדִ֖י לְמֵישָׁרִ֑ים דּוֹבֵ֖ב שִׂפְתֵ֥י יְשֵׁנִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: And the roof of thy mouth like the best wine for my beloved, that goeth down sweetly, causing the lips of those that are asleep to speak.
verses.rendering:
and your mouth like the finest wine —
flowing smoothly for my beloved,
gliding over the lips of sleepers.
translator_notes: Ve-chikkekh ke-yen ha-tov ('and your palate/mouth like the best wine') — her kisses intoxicate like the choicest vintage. The wine 'goes for my beloved smoothly' (holekh le-dodi le-mesharim) — the woman takes over mid-verse, speaking of 'my beloved.' The final image — dovev siftey yeshenim ('causing the lips of sleepers to murmur') — suggests wine so potent it makes even sleeping people speak, or kisses that penetrate even sleep.

verses.text_kjv: I am my beloved's, and his desire is toward me.
verses.rendering:
I belong to my beloved,
and his desire is toward me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The mutual-possession formula reaches its final form. In 2:16: dodi li va-ani lo ('my beloved is mine and I am his') — equal possession. In 6:3: ani le-dodi ve-dodi li ('I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine') — she leads with yielding. Now: ani le-dodi ve-alay teshuqato ('I am my beloved's, and his desire is upon me'). She no longer claims possession at all — she simply belongs to him, and his teshuqah ('desire') flows toward her. The word teshuqah is explosive. It appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible: Genesis 3:16 ('your desire shall be for your husband, and he shall rule over you'), Genesis 4:7 ('sin's desire is for you'), and here. In Genesis 3, desire was bound to domination — the woman desires the man who rules her. In the Song, desire is restored to its pre-fall freedom: his desire is toward her, and there is no rule, no domination, no power struggle. Only longing. The Song undoes the curse.
translator_notes: The teshuqah wordplay with Genesis 3:16 is almost certainly deliberate. The Song presents itself as Eden restored — a garden where desire operates without shame, domination, or curse. His desire is upon her as gravity is upon the earth: natural, constant, joyful.

key_terms.semantic_range: desire, longing, craving; an overwhelming pull toward another
key_terms.note: Teshuqah appears only in Genesis 3:16, 4:7, and here. Its rarity makes each occurrence significant. In Genesis, desire is entangled with sin and domination. In the Song, desire is liberated — it flows freely between lovers without power distortion.

verses.text_hebrew: לְכָ֤ה דוֹדִי֙ נֵצֵ֣א הַשָּׂדֶ֔ה נָלִ֖ינָה בַּכְּפָרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the field; let us lodge in the villages.
verses.rendering:
Come, my beloved, let us go out to the field,
let us spend the night among the henna blossoms.
translator_notes: She takes the initiative: lekhah dodi ('come, my beloved'). She invites him out of the city, into the sadeh ('field, countryside'). Nalinah ba-kefarim can mean 'let us lodge in the villages' or 'let us spend the night among the henna blossoms' (kofer/kefer). The henna reading connects to 1:14 and the Song's fragrance imagery.

verses.text_hebrew: נַשְׁכִּ֙ימָה֙ לַכְּרָמִ֔ים נִרְאֶ֞ה אִם פָּרְחָ֤ה הַגֶּ֙פֶן֙ פִּתַּ֣ח הַסְּמָדַ֔ר הֵנֵ֖צוּ הָרִמּוֹנִ֑ים שָׁ֛ם אֶתֵּ֥ן אֶת־דֹּדַ֖י לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let us get up early to the vineyards; let us see if the vine flourish, whether the tender grape appear, and the pomegranates bud forth: there will I give thee my loves.
translator_notes: The vineyard inspection from 6:11 returns, but now with an explicit purpose: sham etten et doday lakh ('there I will give you my lovemaking'). The vineyard that was 'mine' in 1:6 — the one she had not tended — is now the site of deliberate, chosen lovegiving. She will give him her dodim in the place where love and nature merge.

verses.text_hebrew: הַדּוּדָאִ֣ים נָֽתְנוּ־רֵ֗יחַ וְעַל־פְּתָחֵ֙ינוּ֙ כָּל־מְגָדִ֔ים חֲדָשִׁ֖ים גַּם־יְשָׁנִ֑ים דּוֹדִ֖י צָפַ֥נְתִּי לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: The mandrakes give a smell, and at our gates are all manner of pleasant fruits, new and old, which I have laid up for thee, O my beloved.
verses.rendering:
The mandrakes give off their fragrance,
and at our doors are all choice fruits,
new and old —
my beloved, I have stored them up for you.
translator_notes: Duda'im ('mandrakes') are an aphrodisiac plant with a long history in love magic (Genesis 30:14-16). Their fragrance announces sexual readiness. At 'our doors' (petachenu) — the shared threshold — are kol megadim ('all choice fruits'), both new and old. She has been saving pleasures for him: chadashim gam yeshanim ('new and also old') — fresh experiences and familiar delights.

key_terms.semantic_range: mandrake plant (Mandragora officinarum); an aphrodisiac and fertility aid
key_terms.note: Duda'im shares a root with dodim ('lovemaking') and dod ('beloved'). The plant was widely believed to promote fertility and desire. Its appearance here saturates the scene with erotic intention.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֤י יִתֶּנְךָ֙ כְּאָ֣ח לִ֔י יוֹנֵ֖ק שְׁדֵ֣י אִמִּ֑י אֶֽמְצָאֲךָ֤ בַחוּץ֙ אֶשָּׁ֣קְךָ֔ גַּ֖ם לֹא־יָב֥וּזוּ לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: O that thou wert as my brother, that sucked the breasts of my mother! when I should find thee without, I would kiss thee; yea, I should not be despised.
verses.rendering:
If only you were like a brother to me,
one who nursed at my mother's breasts!
If I found you outside I would kiss you,
and no one would despise me.
translator_notes: Mi yitenkha ke-ach li ('who would make you like a brother to me') — she wishes for the social freedom that siblings enjoy. In the ancient Near East, a sister could kiss her brother publicly without scandal. She wants to express her love openly, without social punishment. The verse exposes the constraint she lives under: their love, though right and mutual, is judged by the world outside.
